name: Maui
description: Maui is a demigod and trickster figure prominent in Polynesian mythology. He is known for his incredible strength, cunning intelligence, and his numerous feats that shaped the world and benefited humanity. Born prematurely and cast into the sea by his mother, Taranga, he was rescued and raised by his grandfather. Upon reuniting with his family, he demonstrated his extraordinary abilities, earning their respect and admiration. Maui is credited with slowing down the sun to make the days longer, fishing up islands from the depths of the ocean, discovering the secret of fire, and attempting to conquer death. His adventures are filled with humor, bravery, and a touch of recklessness. He is a cultural hero celebrated throughout Polynesia, embodying the spirit of ingenuity and the constant struggle against the limitations of the natural world. His stories serve as moral lessons, highlighting the importance of resourcefulness and the consequences of hubris. Maui's legacy continues to inspire and entertain, reminding us of the power of human potential and the enduring allure of the unknown. He is often seen as a bridge between the gods and humanity, using his divine gifts to improve the lives of mortals.
subtitle: The Trickster Demigod of Polynesian Legend

keyMyths.title: Maui Slows the Sun
keyMyths.description: Maui lassoed the sun to slow its journey across the sky, making the days longer for his mother to complete her tasks. He used a rope made from his sister's hair to accomplish this feat.

keyMyths.title: Maui Fishes Up Islands
keyMyths.description: While fishing with his brothers, Maui tricked them and used a magical fishhook to pull up massive islands from the ocean floor. This accounts for the creation of many Polynesian islands.

keyMyths.title: Maui Discovers Fire
keyMyths.description: Maui tricked the mudhens (or other birds, depending on the tradition) into revealing the secret of fire, bringing it to humanity. He accomplished this through cunning and perseverance.

sources.author: Katharine Luomala
sources.work: Maui-of-a-Thousand-Tricks: His Oceanic and European Affiliations
sources.description: A comprehensive study of Maui's stories and their variations across Polynesia.
sources.text: A comprehensive study of Maui's stories and their variations across Polynesia.

sources.author: Margaret Orbell

sources.description: A detailed overview of Māori mythology, including Maui's role and exploits.
sources.text: A detailed overview of Māori mythology, including Maui's role and exploits.

cultural.modernLegacy: Maui's stories continue to be retold in Polynesian communities, often used as educational tools to teach cultural values and traditions. He is also a popular figure in modern media, appearing in films, books, and video games, which has helped to introduce Polynesian mythology to a wider audience.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Maui's birth is shrouded in myth and varies across different Polynesian islands. A common thread is that his mother, Taranga, prematurely gave birth to him and, believing him to be stillborn, cast him into the sea. Miraculously, he was rescued by his grandfather, often referred to as Tama-nui-te-ra (Great Son of the Sun), who raised him in a hidden world. Another variation speaks of Maui being born from his mother's armpit. Regardless of the specific details, Maui's birth is marked by unusual circumstances, foreshadowing his extraordinary destiny. His upbringing by his grandfather instilled in him a deep connection to the natural world and a sense of responsibility to use his powers for the benefit of others. Upon reaching adulthood, Maui sought out his mother and brothers, proving his lineage through displays of his incredible strength and cunning.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: Maui is credited with a multitude of legendary feats that have shaped the world as we know it. One of his most famous accomplishments is slowing down the sun. Frustrated by the sun's rapid journey across the sky, which left little time for work and activities, Maui snared the sun with ropes woven from his sister's hair, forcing it to travel more slowly. Another significant myth involves Maui fishing up islands from the depths of the ocean. Using a magical fishhook (Manaiakalani), he hauled up landmasses that became the islands of Polynesia. He is also credited with discovering fire for humanity. He tricked Mahuika, the goddess of fire, into revealing her secret, allowing humans to access this essential element. These stories highlight Maui's ingenuity, bravery, and his dedication to improving the lives of his people.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Maui's legacy extends far beyond the ancient myths. He remains a central figure in Polynesian culture, embodying qualities of heroism, resourcefulness, and a deep connection to the natural world. His stories are passed down through generations, teaching important lessons about courage, responsibility, and the importance of respecting the environment. Maui's influence can be seen in various art forms, including carvings, tattoos, and traditional dances. His image and stories also appear in contemporary media, such as films and literature, introducing his character to a global audience. While these modern interpretations often take creative liberties, they contribute to the continued celebration and recognition of Maui as a powerful and enduring figure in Polynesian mythology.

shortDescription: A Polynesian demigod and trickster celebrated for his extraordinary feats that shaped the world and benefited humanity.
